coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots synthesized by green method regulate electron transport on the surface of hollow spherical NiCo2S4 for efficient photocatalytic H2 evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Sheng
Xu, Jing
Liu, Zeying
Li, Yanru
coredata.dc:description: An organic-inorganic hybrid material GQDs/NiCo2S4 based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was prepared and applied in the photocatalytic H2 evolution reaction (HER). Based on the NiCo2S4 with a unique hollow spherical structure, the H2 evolution rate of hybrid material GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 with optimal activity reaches 118.14 ​μmol ​h−1 under visible light in EY sensitized system. The electron transport mechanism of GQDs was studied by Mott-Schottky and UV–vis DRS combined with bandgap calculation. The GQDs with low LUMO are sensitizer under visible light excitation. With the sensitization of GQDs, the excited electrons on GQDs are transferred to the surface of NiCo2S4 more rapidly, and the catalytic active sites on the surface of the bimetallic sulfide are activated more effectively while promoting the separation of photogenerated carriers.

GQDs/NiCo2S4
Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots synthesized by green method regulate electron transport on the surface of hollow spherical NiCo2S4 for efficient photocatalytic H2 evolution

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An organic-inorganic hybrid material GQDs/NiCo2S4 based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was prepared and applied in the photocatalytic H2 evolution reaction (HER). Based on the NiCo2S4 with a unique hollow spherical structure, the H2 evolution rate of hybrid material GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 with optimal activity reaches 118.14 ​μmol ​h−1 under visible light in EY sensitized system. The electron transport mechanism of GQDs was studied by Mott-Schottky and UV–vis DRS combined with bandgap calculation. The GQDs with low LUMO are sensitizer under visible light excitation. With the sensitization of GQDs, the excited electrons on GQDs are transferred to the surface of NiCo2S4 more rapidly, and the catalytic active sites on the surface of the bimetallic sulfide are activated more effectively while promoting the separation of photogenerated carriers.

Graphene quantum dots were prepared using an environmentally friendly method and green raw materials. The graphene quantum dots prepared by this way were thermally coupled to the hollow spherical NiCo2S4 surface. Because of the sensitization of GQDs, the composite material GQDs/NiCo2S4 achieved excellent photocatalytic HER activity with great stability.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The realization of energy conversion based on solar energy has become a topic full of opportunities and challenges. In this context, the research on novel inorganic or organic materials to achieve solar-driven photocatalytic HER is paid increasing attention by many researchers [1–3].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a 0D material derived from 2D graphene. The thickness of GQDs is equivalent to one layer or a few layers of graphene, and the lateral size of them is less than 20 ​nm. The adjustable size of GQDs gives them many unique optical properties caused by quantum confinement effects [4,5]. Bandgap width and photo responsibility are important properties of photocatalytic materials. The adjustable bandgap of GQDs gives them a unique advantage in various photocatalytic materials [5]. Therefore, GQDs have been focused on by many researchers in the field of photocatalysis and electro-catalysis [6–8]. Because of the great fluorescence performance, GQDs already have photocatalytic activity themselves. It has been reported the degradation of organic matter and photocatalytic HER are directly realized by modified GQDs [9,10]. Roushani et al. have utilized GQDs to achieve photocatalytic degradation of dye pollutants [9]. Min et al. have prepared N, S codoped graphene quantum dots and introduced Pt as the co-catalyst to achieve homogeneous photocatalytic HER under visible light in ErB sensitized system. And at 420 ​nm, apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of the system is up to 12.3% [10].

The conventional processes of preparing GQDs, such as the Hummers’ method, are very complicated. Those methods always need post-treatment (such as dialysis)to remove non-carbon impurities [4,11]. Given the above factors, in this work, GQDs which were prepared by pyrolysis of starch were introduced into photocatalytic HER. This method to prepare GQDs has been reported by Dai and Chen et al. firstly, and the GQDs have been applied to cell imaging [12]. The GQDs synthesized by this method show great water solubility. Their high photoluminescence emission gives them outstanding optical imaging performance. The most important thing is the method is environmentally friendly and the raw materials are cheap and easy to obtain, which greatly reduces the potential harm to the environment during the preparation of GQDs.

GQDs have the certain photocatalytic ability, however, their catalytic activity is still weak [13]. Therefore, the combination of GQDs with other materials has become one of the main ideas for the application of GQDs in the field of photocatalysis and photoelectrocatalysis [14–16]. Zhang et al. have used GQDs to modify coral-shaped MoS2 to achieve the excellent HER effect [6]. Among many photocatalytic materials, semiconductor materials have been studied extensively. Especially, transition metal sulfide semiconductor materials have attracted much attention in recent years [17,18]. Bimetallic sulfides have more crystal face exposure and better catalytic activity compared with single metal sulfides due to the synergistic effect of two kinds of metal in the crystal lattice [19,20]. Common classical bimetallic sulfides such as ZnxCd1-xS and ZnIn2S4 have been widely applied in the photocatalytic field [21–24]. The bimetallic sulfide NiCo2S4 has been reported in the fields of batteries, supercapacitors, and electrocatalysis [25–29]. Xu et al. have reported NiCo2S4/Co9S8/Ni foam supercapacitors based on NiCo2S4. The asymmetric supercapacitor has excellent property and high energy density which is 49.6 ​Wh.kg−1 ​at a power density of 123 ​W.kg−1 [30]. Jiang et al. have achieved the great overwater splitting by the heterostructure of NiCo2S4@NiFe LDH on foamed nickel [31]. Zhang et al. have prepared hydrangea-like NiCo2S4. The rechargeable zinc/air battery assembled using this material display great discharge performance and stability [32]. However, as a class of semiconductor materials with excellent properties, there is no precedent for a combination of bimetallic sulfides with GQDs and their application in photocatalytic HER. When GQDs are combined with bimetallic sulfides, the mechanism of interaction between them is still unclear.

Herein, the mechanism of green synthetic GQDs coupled with hollow spherical bimetallic sulfide NiCo2S4 in photocatalytic HER was revealed. The hollow spherical NiCo2S4 exhibits great photocatalytic HER activity by its hollow spherical structure and the properties of the bimetallic sulfide. Next, according to the report of Dai and Chen et al., GQDs were successfully prepared by the pyrolysis of starch using the simple hydrothermal method. The particle size of GQDs prepared by this method is about 4–5 ​nm, which makes them own a low potential of its LUMO. Then, the GQDs were coupled to the surface of NiCo2S4 hollow spheres by hydrothermal deposition, the photocatalytic HER activity of NiCo2S4 was effectively improved because of the lower LUMO level of GQDs. In HER, mechanistic studies demonstrated that GQDs play a major role in sensitization and electron transfer.

The reagents including Eosin Y (EY), Co(NO3)2·6H2O, CH4N2S, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and CH3COONa were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Ethylene glycol and Triethanolamine (TEOA) were purchased at Damao chemical reagent factory. And all of them are AR grade. Starch is edible corn starch.

The hollow spherical bimetallic sulfide NiCo2S4 was prepared by a one-step solvothermal method. Specifically, 0.2 ​g CH3COONa, 5 ​mmol CH4N2S, 2 ​mmol Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 1 ​mmol Ni(NO3)2·6H2O were dissolved into 60 ​mL ethylene glycol to get a uniform solution. Then the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined reactor (100 ​mL), and after 12 ​h of reaction at 180 ​°C, it was naturally cooled. The product was washed with deionized water (DW) and ethanol absolute, then maintained at 80 ​°C for 15 ​h. The final product obtained by the above method was the black powder. The solid spherical NiCo2S4 was prepared using the same quality of the above nickel source, cobalt source, and sulfur source without adding CH3COONa.under the same reaction conditions. 60 ​mL DW was used as the solvent, and the same reaction temperature and time were maintained as before.

0.9 ​g starch was dissolved into 60 ​mL DW and stirred at 60 ​°C for 30 ​min to gelatinize the starch. The mixture was transferred into the 100 ​mL Teflon-lined reactor and reacted at 200 ​°C for 4 ​h. After the mixture has cooled naturally, the black impurity in the brown solution was filtered off and the product was the GQDs solution. Fig. S1 displays the GQDs prepared by this method have a great fluorescence phenomenon.

GQDs/NiCo2S4 composite photocatalytic materials were prepared by hydrothermal deposition. In brief, 0.1 ​g NiCo2S4 power was uniformly dispersed in DW in a certain amount of GQDs solution ​(see ​Scheme 1 ). The total volume of GQDs solution and DW is guaranteed to be 75 ​mL. The above mixture was naturally cooled after maintaining at 180 ​°C for 10 ​h in a 100 ​mL Teflon reactor. The liquid was filtered off to give a black powder without additional washing. The product was dried at 80 ​°C for 12 ​h. To prepare different GQDs/NiCo2S4 samples, the GQDs solution was added in an amount of 1 ​mL, 3 ​mL, 5 ​mL, 7 ​mL, and 9 ​mL. The final products were labeled as GQDs/NiCo2S4-1, GQDs/NiCo2S4-3, GQDs/NiCo2S4-5, GQDs/NiCo2S4-7, and GQDs/NiCo2S4-9. In addition, 0.1 ​g NiCo2S4was placed in a 75 ​mL mixed solution (containing 5 ​mL of GQDs solution and 70 ​mL of DW) and stirred for 10 ​h. Then the liquid was filtered off and dried as before. The sample was labeled as GQDs/NiCo2S4–5S.

The crystal structure of the photocatalytic materials characterized by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku D) with Cu Kɑ radiation carried out at 40 ​kV and 40 ​mA (5°/min). ZEISS EVO 18 with an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDX) was used to take the scanning electron microscope (SEM) images. The morphology of GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 was studied using the FEI Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope (TEM). Kratos Axis Ultra DLD using Al Kɑ was utilized to test X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of GQDs/NiCo2S4-5. Nitrogen adsorption isotherm to analyze the BET specific surface area (SBET) was carried out by Micromeritics ASAP 2020. Shimadu UV2550 UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) spectrograph was used to research the optical property of NiCo2S4 and GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 based on the BaSO4. Steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired by the Horiba Scientific FluoroMax-4. Further time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) test was carried out by Horiba Jobin Yvon Data Station HUB. The excitation source of it was an LED diode emitting pulse (370 ​nm, 1 ​MHz repetition rate).

5 ​W LED lamps (λ ​> ​420 ​nm) in a multi-channel system (Perfect, PCX50B) were the light sources in photocatalytic HER. The reaction occurred in a 65 ​mL quartz flask. In brief, there were 0.01 ​g samples and 0.02 ​g EY in a TEOA-H2O solution (10%, v/v, pH ​= ​10) which volume is 30 ​mL to form a uniformly dispersed mixed suspension. Then replace the air in the flask with nitrogen. The composition of the gas in the reaction flask was tested every 0.5 ​h for 3 ​h. Gas component detection was performed by Ruili SP-2100a gas chromatography. The gas chromatography used 13X columns with a TCD detector. At the same time, N2 is the carrier gas. In the stability experiment, the cycle was repeated every 3 ​h. The photocatalytic materials in the reaction solution were filtered every 3 ​h for the experiment to realize the next cycle.

The photoanodes were prepared by ITO glass (1.0 ​cm ​× ​2.0 ​cm) firstly. Specifically, 0.01 ​g different samples were ultrasonically dispersed in 120 ​μL of ethanol absolute (including 10 ​μL of 5% Nafion) to form a slurry mixture. The mixture was evenly distributed over the conductive surface of the ITO glass and the coated area was 1.0 ​cm2. Then the ITO glasses were dried at 80 ​°C for 12 ​h to obtain the work electrodes. Chen Hua CHI660E electrochemical workstation was used as the electrochemical test device. The reference electrode was an Ag/AgCl electrode, and a platinum disk working electrode was used as the counter electrode. 0.2 ​mol L−1 Na2SO4 aqueous solution was the electrolyte. The electrochemical reaction was carried out in a three-electrode cell. The transient photocurrent response test (I-T) was performed under a 300 ​W xenon lamp (λ ​> ​420 ​nm) with a bias of 0.5 ​V. The Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) test maintained 0.5 ​V bias with the scan rate of 10 ​mV S−1. The frequency of the Mott-Schottky experiment was 1 ​kHz.

At 2θ ​= ​21°, there is a diffraction peak which is corresponding to the layer spacing of carbon material that can be observed in Fig. 1 (a). Similar to the previous reports, the diffraction peak shows a diffuse peak shape caused by the small size of GQDs [33]. The XRD pattern strongly proves that GQDs were successfully synthesized by pyrolysis of starch at high temperature [12]. Fig. 1 (b) is the XRD patterns of NiCo2S4 and GQDs/NiCo2S4-5. In the XRD pattern of NiCo2S4, at 2θ ​= ​26.83°, 31.59°, 38.32°, 47.41°, and 55.33°, significant diffraction peaks are shown. These diffraction peaks belong to JCPDS Card No. 20-0782. The definite XRD diffraction result assigns to the cubic phase of NiCo2S4 [25]. The XRD pattern of NiCo2S4 shows the solvothermal method can effectively synthesize NiCo2S4 crystal material. In addition, compared with the XRD pattern of NiCo2S4, the XRD pattern of GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 does not change significantly. This indicates that the coupling of GQDs to the surface of NiCo2S4 by thermal coupling has no significant effect on the crystallinity of NiCo2S4. Since the GQDs coupled to the surface of NiCo2S4 have small size and randomly disperse the surface of NiCo2S4, the diffraction peak belonging to GQDs cannot be observed [33].

From Fig. 2 (a), GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 exhibits a hollow spherical shape whose diameter is about 500–600 ​nm. As shown in Fig. S2, hydrothermal method makes NiCo2S4 display a distinct solid spherical shape. Fig. S3 displays that the hollow spherical structure gives GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 better dispersion in aqueous solution, which is very advantageous for heterogeneous photocatalytic HER [34]. From Fig. 2(b), the energy dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDX) corresponding to the SEM of GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 is displayed. The presence of Ni, Co, S and C elements is evident from it.

In the TEM image of GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 (Fig. 3 ), spotted GQDs were uniformly distributed on the surface of hollow spherical NiCo2S4. Further, as shown in Fig. 3(a) which is a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) photograph of GQDs/NiCo2S4-5, the diameter of GQDs dispersed on the surface of NiCo2S4 is about 4–5 ​nm. It is worth noting that in Fig. 3(b), the clear lattice fringes belonging to the GQDs (1 0 0) facet can be observed, and the corresponding interplanar spacing is 0.21 ​nm [35–39]. This further testifies that GQDs prepared by pyrolysis of starch have great crystallinity. Also, the interplanar spacing of 0.27 ​nm corresponds to the (2 2 2) facet of NiCo2S4 [28,29]. The HRTEM image strongly characterizes the great coupling of GQDs on the surface of NiCo2S4.

FT-IR spectra were further used to characterize the photocatalytic materials. It is observed in the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 4 ) the peaks at 1654 ​nm and 1407 ​nm show a skeleton vibration similar to the aromatic ring between the carbon atoms belonging to GQDs, which is similar to the previous reports. This corresponds to the hexagonal structure formed by sp2 hybridization of carbon atoms in GQDs [12]. FT-IR spectrum strongly proves GQDs were successfully synthesized. The peaks at 3440 ​nm, 2929 ​nm, and 1053 ​nm correspond to the stretching vibration absorption of the O–H, C–H, and C–O bonds, respectively [14,33]. This indicates the GQDs prepared by this method have abundant oxygen-containing functional groups. For the FT-IR spectrum of NiCo2S4, in addition to the peaks corresponding to the O–H and C–O, it is observed the stretching vibration absorption belongs to SO [40,41]. This corresponds to the small amount of sulfate shown in the S 2p region in XPS. In the FT-IR spectrum of the composite, the peaks corresponding to the unique functional groups of NiCo2S4 and GQDs can be observed simultaneously. This strongly proves the simultaneous existence of GQDs and NiCo2S4 in the composite material.

To further explore the reasons for the strong coupling between GQDs and the surface of NiCo2S4, the way of connection between them was studied using XPS. As seen in Fig. 5 (a), GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 has Ni, Co, S and C elements, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of each element were deconvolved using Gaussian-Lorentzian product function. In Fig. 5(e), the peak belonging to C 1s is divided into four sub-peaks. One of the peaks at 284.7eV belongs to contaminated carbon for data correction, and the other part belongs to CC for GQDs. In addition, the peaks corresponding to COOH, C–O, and CO testify that the surface of GQDs prepared by thermally decomposing starch via high temperature has a partial oxygen-containing functional group [14]. The oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of GQDs provide the possibility for GQDs to form chemical bonds with NiCo2S4. Under high-temperature hydrothermal conditions, the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of GQDs are bonded to NiCo2S4 to obtain a strong coupling effect. The main peak of Ni 2p is divided into six sub-peaks by a deconvolution operation. The two spin splitting peaks which are located at 853.3eV and 870.2eV prove the existence of Ni2+. The double peaks at 856.5eV and 875.3eV attest that the nickel element also appears in the form of Ni3+ in the crystal lattice. Furthermore, the double peaks located at 861.4eV and 879.6eV imply the existence of the corresponding satellite peak (Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2) [26]. There are 4 sub-peaks in the Co 2p region. For one thing, the double peaks at 778.6eV and 780.3eV correspond to Co3+ [26]. For another, the spin splitting peaks which are located at 794.8eV and 796.5eV are attributed to Co2+ [26]. According to previous reports, the different valence states coexisting in the crystal lattice of Ni and Co elements prove the bimetallic sulfide NiCo2S4 was successfully synthesized. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of the S 2p is similar to the previous reports [42–45]. The S element is mainly in the form of S2− [46] in the excessive metal sulfide lattice, corresponding to the double peaks at 160.9eV and 161.9eV. Furthermore, the peaks of the S 2p at 163.1eV and 164.1eV imply there is S2 2− ligand on the surface of NiCo2S4, whose amount is small [47]. Pair of peaks appearing at 167.8eV and 168.8eV is attributed to sulfate. The presence of sulfate should be attributed to partial pyrolysis of thiourea in the high-temperature environment, which is similar to previous reports [46,48]. XPS narrow spectrum of O 1s shows many different forms of oxygen exist in the system. The single peaks at 533 ​eV, 532 ​eV, and 531.6 ​eV are mainly associated with the presence of OC [49], O–C [50,51], and O–H [52], respectively. In addition, the singlet at 532 ​eV also corresponds to the inorganic SO [53]. And the broad peak at 531.6 ​eV corresponds to a small number of oxidation states of Ni and Co [54–58]. OC, O–C and O–H come from the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of GQDs. The inorganic SO corresponds to the sulfate in the S 2p region of XPS. The existence of oxidation states of Ni and Co (Ni–O and Co–O) is owing to the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of GQDs and the Ni and Co atoms on the surface of NiCo2S4 are connected through chemical bonds. This achieves a strong coupling between the surfaces of them. On this basis, GQDs were successfully coupled to the surface of NiCo2S4 to form composite GQDs/NiCo2S4-5. Table S1 shows the approximate elemental composition of the surface of the composite material. The approximate contents of C, O, S, Ni and Co elements are 26.20 ​wt%, 29.92 ​wt%, 14.88 ​wt%, 7.38 ​wt% and 21.61 ​wt%, respectively .

The N2 isothermal (77 ​K) adsorption-desorption curves of NiCo2S4 and GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 are displayed in Fig. 6 (a). It is obvious that both they are type IV isotherms and accompanied by the characteristics of the H3 hysteresis loop. This manifests the surface of NiCo2S4 hollow spheres has a rich pore structure. Combined with the SBET parameter in Table 1
, it is obvious that the SBET of GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 (41.67 ​m2 g−1) is slightly larger than that of NiCo2S4 (36.83 ​m2 g−1). This should be attributed to the coupling of GQDs on the surface of NiCo2S4-5. The pore volume data in Table 1 corresponds to SBET. Surface-coupled GQDs increase the SBET of the NiCo2S4 hollow spheres while also increasing the pore volume of it. The average pore size data corresponds to the BJH pore size distribution curves in Fig. 6(b). The average pore size of the NiCo2S4 hollow spheres is 8.21 ​nm, which is slightly larger than that of GQDs/NiCo2S4-5. The HRTEM of GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 shows the GQDs coupled to the surface of NiCo2S4 have a particle size of about 4–5 ​nm. When GQDs are coupled to the surface of NiCo2S4, a part of the pores on the surface of NiCo2S4 are masked by GQDs, so that the average pore size calculated by the N2 isothermal adsorption experiment on the surface of GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 is slightly reduced. The above analysis further proves the hydrothermal deposition method can successfully couple GQDs to the surface of NiCo2S4 hollow spheres.

Primarily, the concentration of GQDs during hydrothermal deposition has a remarkable influence on the HER activity of the composite GQDs/NiCo2S4. With the increasing of the concentration of GQDs, the photocatalytic HER activity of different composite materials shows a trend of strengthening firstly and then weakening in Fig. 7 (a). When 5 ​mL GQDs solution is added to the mixed solution, the composite exhibits the best photocatalytic HER activity. The H2 evolution rate of GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 reaches 118.14 ​μmol ​h−1 in Fig. 7(b). When the amount of GQDs solution added during hydrothermal deposition is less than 5 ​mL, only a small amount of GQDs can be coupled to the surface of NiCo2S4. However, when the amount of GQDs solution is higher than 5 ​mL, the HER rate of the system is significantly decreased. This shows excessive GQDs deposition reduces the photocatalytic HER activity of the composite, which is similar to the previous reports [33]. This should be owing to excessive GQDs on the surface of NiCo2S4 block the light absorption of NiCo2S4, which lowers the photoelectron concentration on NiCo2S4 and decreases the photocatalytic activity of HER. In addition, excessive GQDs cover the surface of the NiCo2S4 hollow spheres. This isolates the contact between NiCo2S4 and EY molecules. It is difficult for electrons generated by EY to be transported to the surface of NiCo2S4, which may also cause this phenomenon.

Secondly, the effect of pH change on HER activity of GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 during H2 evolution was discussed. Fig. 8 (a) shows the photocatalytic HER rate of GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 at different pH. Obviously, as the pH of the reaction environment increases, the photocatalytic rate tends to rise first and then decrease. When pH ​= ​10, the system has the highest HER rate. In particular, when GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 is in a highly alkaline environment, the HER rate of the system is significantly reduced. As long as the concentration of H+ is lowered in a strongly alkaline environment, GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 under EY sensitization is insufficient to reduce sufficient H+, thereby showing that the photocatalytic activity is weakened [59]. Finally, the effect of the EY concentration in the HER rate of the reaction system was investigated. In Fig. 8(b) it’s obvious that the system owns the optimal photocatalytic HER rate when 0.02 ​g EY is added in the reaction solution (0.96 ​mmol.L−1). This is mainly since electrons are most efficiently transported to GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 after being excited when EY in this concentration is adsorbed on GQDs/NiCo2S4-5. In the EY concentration of the reaction environment below 0.96 ​mmol.L−1, EY molecules are not saturated by the adsorption of the composite. However, in the environment where the EY concentration is higher than 0.96 ​mmol.L−1, a shielding effect of the dye occurs [60]. The above factors cause the photocatalytic HER rate of GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 to decrease in the EY sensitization system.

From Fig. 9 , both NiCo2S4 and GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 exhibit great photocatalytic stability. Among them, NiCo2S4 shows a slight decrease in photocatalytic HER activity after the 4 cycles of stability experiments. This should be owing to the slight photo-etching of transition metal sulfides. The photocatalytic HER activity of GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 also decreased slightly. For one thing, this is attributed to the photo-etching of NiCo2S4. For another, a small amount of GQDs shedding may also cause the decrease. In addition, GQDs/NiCo2S4–5S prepared by physical stirring displayed poor photocatalytic stability during the stability tests. Especially, in the fourth cycle experiment, the amount of H2 evolution within 3 ​h was very close to that of NiCo2S4. This demonstrates the GQDs on the surface of GQDs/NiCo2S4–5S are largely detached due to illumination and physical agitation during the stability experiments, and finally the photocatalytic activity of HER is decreased. The above experimental results show the hydrothermal deposition method can uniformly and stably couple GQDs to the surface of NiCo2S4 under high temperature and high pressure conditions. To a great extent, the great activity and stability of GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 during the photocatalytic HER are benefited from the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of GQDs [33]. GQDs and NiCo2S4 were chemically combined to form an effective strong coupling during the hydrothermal process.

As shown in Fig. 10 (a), it is obvious that the light absorption property of NiCo2S4 is enhanced by surface-coupled GQDs. Tauc plots were performed on the data in the UV–vis DRS spectra to calculate the bandgaps of NiCo2S4 and GQDs/NiCo2S4-5. In Fig. 10(b), NiCo2S4 has a bandgap of 2.50eV. In addition, the loading of GQDs has not changed the bandgap of NiCo2S4 significantly. This manifests the enhanced photocatalytic activity of composites is due to the enhancement of full-wavelength light absorption by GQDs coupled to the surface of NiCo2S4, rather than directly changing the light-absorbing performance of a certain band.

Through the steady-state fluorescence tests, the electron transfer mechanism of different photocatalytic materials in the EY-sensitized photocatalytic HER system was studied. Steady-state PL tests were performed on the TEOA-H2O solution (10%, v/v, pH ​= ​10) containing EY (1 ​× ​10−6 ​mol.L−1) without the addition of a photocatalyst firstly. As shown in Fig. 11 (a), when there is only EY in the TEOA-H2O solution, a strong emission signal appears at 538 ​nm with the excitation of a stable light source of 480 ​nm. Next, when the NiCo2S4 hollow spheres with a concentration of 0.2 ​mg mL−1 were added to the solution, the intensity of the photoluminescence emission peak at 538 ​nm was significantly weakened. This is due to the fluorescence quenching between the excited EY molecules adsorbed on the NiCo2S4 hollow spheres [61]. Subsequently, when the photocatalytic material in the solution was GQDs/NiCo2S4-5, the emission peak intensity was further weakened, resulting in the more intense fluorescence quenching phenomenon. Enhanced fluorescence quenching testifies that GQDs coupled to the surface of NiCo2S4 can effectively inhibit the recombination between photogenerated carriers.

The fluorescence decay curves in Fig. 11(b) were fitted by a double-exponential fluorescence decay model to obtain the data which was displayed in Table 2. And the lifetime of different samples was obtained as follows [62]:

When the TEOA-H2O solution was added to 1 ​× ​10−6 ​mol L−1 EY, a fluorescent species with an average lifetime of 0.137ns appeared in the solution with the excitation from the LED light. And the single exponential fluorescence model indicates that the fluorescent species is one. When NiCo2S4 powder (0.2 ​mg ​mL−1) and EY were simultaneously present in the solution, fluorescence quenching between the excited EY molecule and the NiCo2S4 surface extended the average lifetime of the fluorescent species in the solution to 0.259 ns. In addition, the double-exponential fluorescence decay model implies that two kinds of fluorescent species exist simultaneously, and the fluorescence quenching between the excited EY molecule and NiCo2S4 is dynamic. When GQDs were coupled to the surface of NiCo2S4, the enhanced dynamic quenching between the formed GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 and the excited EY molecule further prolonged the average lifetime (0.321 ns) of the fluorescent species in the solution. The results of TRPL tests further prove that when GQDs are coupled to the surface of NiCo2S4, the photogenerated carriers are effectively promoted, and the average lifetime of photogenerated electrons is prolonged [62].

The transfer mechanism of charges on GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 was studied by I-T and LSV tests. As shown in Fig. 12 (a), there is no observed photo-generated current on the ITO glass without the photocatalytic materials under the illumination and applied bias. When ITO glass with NiCo2S4 coated is used as a photoanode, the significant photo-generated current is generated along with the illumination. This testifies that NiCo2S4 has a great photocurrent response. Further, when the photoanode is ITO glass with GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 coated, the photocurrent density is remarkably enhanced under the same illumination intensity and applied bias. This proves that GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 has a stronger photocurrent response which corresponds to the results of the photocatalytic HER experiments. The results of I-T tests show GQDs coupled to the surface of NiCo2S4 can accelerate the transfer of photogenerated electrons, resulting in a stronger photocurrent response.

Fig. 12(b) shows the LSV curves of different photocatalysts. Similar to the I-T curves, when the photoanode is only ITO glass, the cathode current accompanying the applied bias is not observed. However, after NiCo2S4 is coated on the ITO glass, a significant cathode current is generated with the application of an applied bias, indicating the occurrence of HER. Subsequently, for the GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 coated ITO glass photoanode, the lower overpotential is observed from its corresponding LSV curve. This strongly proves GQDs reduce the overpotential of HER, making HER more prone to occur.

The flat band potential (Efb) of NiCo2S4 was investigated by Mott-Schottky tests. The Mott-Schottky plot of NiCo2S4 shows it is p-type semiconductor materials, and the Efb is close to the conduction band potential (ECB). As shown in Fig. S4, the Efb of NiCo2S4 is −0.41eV (VS.NHE). After attaching GQDs on the surface, NiCo2S4 shows a significant decrease in Efb (−0.49eV) Therefore, the GQDs coupled to the surface of NiCo2S4 give the composite a lower conduction band potential, thereby giving the composite a superior HER activity.

Combined with the bandgap calculation results of UV–vis DRS, conduction band (ECB) and valence band (EVB) values of NiCo2S4 were calculated using empirical formulas [63,64] (the calculation process obtained from the empirical formulas were detailed in electronic supporting documents). The calculated results display the value of conduction band for NiCo2S4 is −0.42eV and the valence band is 2.07eV. The theoretical calculation results are very close to those of the Mott-Schottky experiments, which further verifies the accuracy of the Mott-Schottky experimental results.

According to the above photoelectrochemical tests of different samples and the PL test results, the photocatalytic HER mechanism of GQDs/NiCo2S4-5 is explained as follows in Fig. 13 . The HOMO and LUMO levels of GQDs vary with their particle size. According to previous reports, when the particle size of GQDs is about 4–5 ​nm, its LUMO level of GQDs is about −2.6 ​eV and the HOMO level is 1.15 ​eV [65]. This value of the LUMO of GQDs is much lower than the conduction band potential of NiCo2S4. GQDs are excited by the visible light and the electrons are quickly transferred to the NiCo2S4 surface from their LUMO. At the same time, EY molecules are adsorbed on the surface of GQDs/NiCo2S4-5, and EY molecules absorb the photon energy transition to become EY1∗ under the irradiation of a stable light source. Subsequently, the single-excited state of EY1∗ spontaneously transitions to the more stable excited state of EY3∗. At this time, TEOA and EY3∗ in the reaction solution undergo a redox reaction. TEOA gives electrons and reduces EY3∗ to EY−•, simultaneously forming the oxidation state TEOA+ [66]. EY−• has a lower reduction potential, which is much lower than the conduction band potential of NiCo2S4 and LUMO level of GQDs. This allows photogenerated charges to be rapidly transferred from EY−• to the surface of NiCo2S4 and GQDs. The surface of NiCo2S4 aggregates a large number of electrons transmitted by GQDs and EY−• to achieve HER. In this process, GQDs and EY molecules work together to realize photosensitization and GQDs also pass the electrons from EY−• to NiCo2S4. In addition, the GQDs coupled to the surface of NiCo2S4 reduce the recombination of photogenerated carriers, which prolongs the lifetime of photogenerated electrons. Meanwhile, surface-coupled GQDs can effectively reduce the HER overpotential, allowing the composite to have higher photocatalytic HER activity.

The hollow spherical bimetallic sulfide NiCo2S4 and GQDs synthesized using green raw materials and methods were coupled together to form a hollow spherical organic-inorganic hybrid material and introduced into photocatalytic HER. The process for preparing GQDs by thermally decomposing starch is environmentally friendly. HRTEM shows the particle size of GQDs in this manner is about 4–5 ​nm, which has a low LUMO level. The hollow spherical NiCo2S4 has a unique advantage in heterogeneous photocatalytic HER due to its effective dispersion. On this basis, GQDs were coupled to the surface of NiCo2S4 by simple thermal deposition, which formed a close interaction between them. The photocatalytic activity of the composite is significantly higher than that of the hollow spherical NiCo2S4 alone. Mott-Schottky and UV–vis DRS experiments revealed the mechanism of action of GQDs in photocatalytic HER. The low LUMO content of GQDs greatly enhances the photocatalytic activity of HER. In this process, GQDs acts as a sensitizer and electron transport medium with the overpotential of HER reducing. Furthermore, the coupling of the two materials also promotes the separation of the photo-generated carrier, thereby further enhancing the HER activity of the composite. Using the environmentally friendly method to prepare photocatalytic materials provides a richer idea for the extensive application of photocatalytic materials.
Sheng Zhao: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft. Jing Xu: Formal analysis. Zeying Liu: Investigation. Yanru Li: Investigation.
